original_hypothesis.title: ProxyOracle can be set to a non-contract or incompatible oracle, causing system-wide mispricing/DoS for dependent markets
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ProxyOracle.changeOracleImplementation allows owner to point to any address with no validation (code size check, interface compliance). If set to EOA, zero address, or an oracle that reverts/returns extreme values, all consumers (e.g., Cauldrons using ProxyOracle) can be bricked or manipulated (incorrect exchange rates leading to unsafe borrows or mass liquidations).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete configuration footgun that can have catastrophic downstream effects. While owner action is required (LOW realism under the given model), validating guardrails (e.g., require(address(newOracle).code.length>0), sanity bounds) is important because mistakes happen and upgrade keys get compromised.
deployment_assumptions: ProxyOracle is used as oracle for a Cauldron/market holding user funds
Owner sets oracleImplementation incorrectly or is compromised
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: ProxyOracle.sol changeOracleImplementation has no validation; no deployment scripts provided for ProxyOracle wiring.

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner can set oracleImplementation to arbitrary address without code/interface checks; all oracle calls directly delegate to oracleImplementation

preconditions: ProxyOracle is referenced by critical protocol components
oracleImplementation can be changed by owner
No external monitoring/guard prevents bad updates

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: DoS or mispricing of collateral/borrow calculations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No code length check, no sanity check on returned rates

analysis_questions: Which markets depend on ProxyOracle and what is the blast radius?
Are there operational controls (timelock, multisig, monitoring) outside code?
Could an attacker set oracleImplementation to a contract that returns manipulated rates only for certain data payloads?
validation_requirements: Trace usage of ProxyOracle in deployment configs
Simulate setting implementation to EOA/zero and observe downstream behavior
Add invariant tests for oracle return bounds and non-reverting behavior in critical paths

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised owner points oracleImplementation to malicious oracle that inflates collateral prices; attacker borrows against inflated collateral and leaves bad debt.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential protocol insolvency in markets relying on the oracle
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (can update oracle back, but bad debt may remain)

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for invalid oracle implementation addresses

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
